customer_goal: Make rich-output Gaussian rendering faster for vectorized, independently moving robotics cameras.

verdict.absolute_vectorized_performance: PASS: at physical B8 the custom renderer is faster than pinned gsplat on both full Home resolutions and on the held-out Garage scene.
verdict.proportional_batch_scaling: FAIL: throughput is nearly flat from physical B1 through B8/B16 on Home and rises only 1.254x from B1 to B8 on Garage.
verdict.usefulness: The renderer is useful when a robotics workload needs GPU-resident RGB, depth, alpha, and semantic IDs for large Gaussian scenes under a constrained memory budget. It is not evidence of linear vectorization scaling.
verdict.claim_boundary: These changing-camera comparisons are against pinned gsplat, not OVRTX or the proprietary RTX renderer. The existing 103.397x OVRTX README result uses a separate cache-heavy multi-RenderProduct protocol and must not be presented as a changing-camera speedup.
verdict.optimization_result: First-pass projected-record reuse is the only retained kernel-path optimization from this investigation. Seven additional kernel/layout candidates were neutral or slower and were reverted.
verdict.remaining_work: The dominant compact projection kernel still consumes about 53 percent of P8/P16 kernel time. A future optimization must beat the frozen ABBA control, preserve all-output fidelity, and pass sanitizer and Isaac integration gates.

source.renderer_source_note: No renderer source under src/ changed between the standalone matrix commit and the final renderer commit; later commits improve the benchmark contract, packaging, and Isaac verification.
source.evidence_preservation: Raw JSON, logs, Nsight Systems reports/SQLite/CSV, sanitizer output, driver provenance, and Isaac output were copied off the disposable GPU VM before deletion.

machine.license: NVIDIA RTX Virtual Workstation, licensed permanently for the life of the VM
machine.gsp_firmware: disabled as required by the GRID-on-GCP configuration

protocol.home: Full 21,497,908-Gaussian Home Scan; one logical observation contains 1,024 distinct cameras exactly once; all 1,024 cameras change between both measured observations; bounded physical microbatches P1/P8/P16; no projection cache; full GPU RGB, expected depth, alpha, and semantic-ID outputs; JIT, scene load, warmup, capacity growth, and validation outside measured ranges.
protocol.garage: Held-out full 13,943,336-Gaussian Garage High Detail scene; all 72 source views; independently changing cameras; B1/B2/B4/B8; two fresh processes per renderer and batch; full GPU RGB, expected depth, alpha, and semantic-ID outputs.
protocol.timing: CUDA-event and synchronized-wall timing around the complete render observation. Reported throughput below is CUDA-event images/s; wall throughput was effectively identical for the standalone Home matrix.
protocol.semantic_policy: Both renderers are compared with semantic foreground defined by alpha >= 0.01. This avoids counting gsplat IDs in effectively transparent pixels as semantic disagreement.
comparison_limits: Custom and gsplat use the same deterministic screen-space EWA equation family, so strict same-equation fidelity is meaningful.
OVRTX perspective uses a different stochastic 3D-ray model and was not timed in this changing-camera investigation.
The Isaac integration case is a finite lifecycle and data-path proof, not a large-scene performance benchmark.

scaling.verdict: No proportional vectorization scaling. Larger physical batches mainly increase workspace and reduce native submission count.

gsplat_core_control.purpose: Remove gsplat semantic rendering to test whether the full-output win is solely semantic overhead. This is an unmatched RGB/depth/alpha-only control; custom still emits all four outputs.
gsplat_core_control.interpretation: The large full-output speedup is partly affected by gsplat's semantic-output cost, but a substantial win remains when that cost is removed.

profile.case: Full Home Scan, logical B1024, 128x128, two measured moving-camera states; raw report, SQLite, CSV, and parsed JSON retained for P1/P8/P16.
pipeline: compact project and accumulate conservative depth information
emit retained intersections from cached projected records
global CUB radix sort using multiple kernels

rejected_candidates.decision: reverted as indistinguishable from process variation; strict fidelity was not run
reverted as indistinguishable from process variation; strict fidelity was not run
reverted as indistinguishable from process variation; strict fidelity was not run

rejected_candidates.candidate: dense projection before depth tau accumulation
dense projection storing only pixel-contributing records

isaac_integration.purpose: Prove the real custom CUDA extension builds and runs inside Isaac Sim with Fabric camera ingestion and physics-coupled motion. This small case is not used as a large-scene speed claim.

integration_gaps_fixed.gap: setuptools was absent from the runtime dependencies even though torch.utils.cpp_extension imports it
Ninja was required for clean native JIT builds but was not declared
the Isaac extension smoke could lose the original exception during SimulationApp.close()
the official Isaac Sim 6.0.1 runtime image has no native CUDA build toolchain
integration_gaps_fixed.fix: add a pinned multi-stage CUDA 12.8 development image

integration_gaps_fixed.gap: the first derived build image accidentally ended as root, which Kit rejects
integration_gaps_fixed.ownership: introduced during this investigation, not an original renderer defect
